stem: What does the Ernst angle optimize?

options.text: Steady-state signal for a tissue with a given T1 and repetition time
The shortest possible T2 for every tissue
The maximum gradient strength before quenching
The chemical shift between fat and water

explanation: The Ernst angle balances signal used by each excitation against longitudinal recovery before the next one. Its optimum is tissue- and TR-dependent rather than universal.

stem: For a fixed tissue T1, how does increasing TR affect the Ernst angle?

options.text: The optimal flip angle becomes smaller
The optimal flip angle becomes larger
The optimal flip angle remains zero
The optimal flip angle becomes independent of recovery

explanation: Longer TR permits more longitudinal recovery, so a larger fraction can be tipped without excessive steady-state saturation. At very short TR values, a smaller flip can produce more steady-state signal than a larger one.

objective: Relate T1 to optimal flip angle.
stem: At a fixed short TR, which tissue generally has the larger Ernst angle?

options.text: The tissue with the longer T1
Both tissues because Ernst angle is always 90 degrees
The tissue with the shorter T1
Neither tissue because flip angle affects only T2

explanation: Short-T1 tissue replenishes longitudinal magnetization more rapidly between pulses and can support a larger optimal flip. Long-T1 tissue remains more saturated at the same TR.

stem: Why can a small flip angle produce more steady-state signal than a 90-degree pulse when TR is very short?

options.text: It eliminates the need for transverse magnetization
It doubles proton density inside the voxel
It lengthens TR without changing the sequence timing
It preserves longitudinal magnetization for subsequent excitations

explanation: A 90-degree pulse exhausts the available longitudinal component and severe saturation follows when recovery time is short. A smaller flip sacrifices some immediate signal but leaves magnetization for repeated acquisitions.

stem: Which pair directly determines flip angle for a rectangular RF pulse?

options.text: RF field amplitude and pulse duration
Field of view and phase matrix
Echo time and receiver bandwidth
Slice thickness and number of averages

explanation: Flip angle is proportional to the time integral of B1, so increasing amplitude or duration increases the rotation. Imaging geometry and readout settings do not directly set the excitation angle.
